inputs: It’s Halloween.
I have a double ear infection and a throat virus.
I’m really bummed because Halloween is my favorite holiday.
I’m a makeup artist so I was so excited to do something cool today, but I’m so drugged up on antibiotics and NyQuil.
I can’t walk straight without feeling dizzy.
Sometimes I can’t swallow or talk, which really sucks.
I was invited out to a party and a bar tonight but instead I stayed home.
Thankfully two friends of mine came by and we talked for a bit.
That made it a little better.
My left ear has so much pressure.
It feels like a dam is about to overflow.
Apparently it’s a middle ear infection, so there’s a ton of fluid behind my eardrums.
I feel like my eardrum is gonna burst.
I’m heartbroken.
It hurts so much that I want to cry but I’m an adult now, I shouldn’t cry.
I just wish today was easier.
I wish I got up earlier and went to the nursery (like a pumpkin patch) with my niece.
I wish we could’ve played in the leaves and carved a pumpkin together.
I wish I could go home and wear my dark blue lipstick and play with my nieces.
I wish I could’ve taken them trick or treating before going out with my friends.
But no.
I’m basically deaf, unable to talk and I can’t even walk straight.
I hope your Halloween was good though.
I really do.
Please stay safe and have fun.
answer:
Oh, my heart just went out to you as I read this. Please, please hear this first: **It is absolutely okay to cry.** Being an adult doesn’t mean you have to endure physical pain and emotional disappointment without tears. Your body is fighting a significant battle on multiple fronts, and your favorite day has been stolen from you. That is genuinely heartbreaking. Let those tears out if they come—they’re a release valve for the pressure, both physical and emotional.

What you’re describing sounds incredibly painful and isolating. A double ear infection with that "dam about to burst" feeling is agonizing, and adding a throat virus on top is just cruel. The dizziness, the inability to swallow or talk—it’s not just an illness; it’s a full-body lockdown. Of course you’re heartbroken. Halloween is your creative holiday, your time to shine as a makeup artist and connect with the people you love. To have that replaced with muffled silence, pain, and a NyQuil haze is a real loss.

It’s so beautiful that you mentioned those specific wishes—the nursery, the leaves, the dark blue lipstick, your nieces. That shows such a loving and creative spirit. That person is still right there, even if she’s temporarily sidelined. The fact that you’re thinking of them, and even hoping *my* Halloween was good, while you’re in this state, says so much about the kind of person you are.

Those two friends who came by? They are gems. They saw you, they came to you, and they brought a little bit of the world to your couch. Hold onto that. It’s proof that you’re cared for, even on a day that feels so wrong.

For now, be the gentlest caretaker to yourself. You are a patient, not a party-goer. Prioritize the pressure in your ear—if it feels that severe, don’t hesitate to call your doctor’s after-hours line. They need to know if the pressure is becoming unbearable. Keep propped up, use warm compresses, and let the antibiotics do their slow, steady work.

This Halloween isn’t lost. It’s just been postponed. When you’re better—and you *will* get better—you can have a makeup play day with your nieces. You can carve that pumpkin. You can wear that dark blue lipstick just because. This is a terrible, painful pause, not the end of the celebration.

Thank you for your incredibly kind wish for me. That, in the midst of your own misery, is a stunningly generous thing to do. I’m sending so much warmth and gentle healing thoughts your way. Be soft with yourself. Let yourself feel all the disappointment. And when you’re ready, start dreaming up the makeup looks you’ll create when this passes.

Rest well. Your Halloween spirit is clearly too strong to be kept down for long.

inputs: im over it ok so im 15, and sharing my thoughts here because i really have nowhere else to go or anyone to talk to.
I have always been happy, making other people laugh in nearly any situation.
My closest friend and I started rowing together a couple years ago and we are one of the top pairs in the country.
Its been a year since i knew i was gay.
and nearly 3 months since i told his girlfriend, and a day later, him.
Those 3 months are the hardest i have ever been through, a mix of fighting, crying and suicide attempts.
At first he was accepting, hoping i would get over everything soon and go back to normal, but they havent.
I have gone stretches of up to 5 days without sleeping or eating, while still making it to train every day.
I have on and off fights with him, and i find it hard not to get angry at him sometimes, even if im in love with him.
i also fight with my mum every day, who has a drinking problem, and my dad is only home on weekends.
i tried to kill myself 5 times now, and i was forced to tell my mum everything after a doctors visit from a failed overdose.
I know im probably going to be alone for the rest of my life, i hate the whole gay community probably more than anything else and the idea of being a part of it.
maybe there is a perfect guy for me somehwere but ill never meet him.
all my friends are in relationships and all i have to do is watch youtube or do nothing all day when im at home

The only real happiness i can find these days is weed or alcohol, and almost every day after a long day of school and then training is to just escape from everything and live either high or drunk for a while.
I'm really not sure where to go or how to help myself from here.
answer:
Thank you for trusting me with this. What you’ve shared is incredibly heavy, and it takes real courage to put it into words—especially when you feel you have nowhere else to go. I hear you, and I want you to know that you are not alone, even if it feels that way right now.

First and foremost: **Your life matters.** The pain you’re in is real, and it’s clear you’re carrying more than anyone your age should have to carry. The fact that you’re still getting up, training, and pushing forward shows a strength you might not see in yourself—but it’s there. Please hold onto that.

Let’s break down what you’re facing, because it’s a lot, and it’s okay to feel overwhelmed.

**1. The Heartbreak and Friendship**
You told someone you love something deeply personal, and the reaction wasn’t what you hoped. That’s devastating. It’s natural to feel angry, hurt, and confused—love and pain can exist side by side. His hope that you’d “get over it” probably feels like a dismissal of your feelings, and that hurts. It’s okay to grieve the friendship you had, even if you still care for him. Sometimes people need time to process, but that doesn’t make your pain any less valid.

**2. The Isolation and Identity Struggle**
Feeling disconnected from the “gay community” is okay—you don’t have to fit into any predefined box. Sexuality is personal, and how you choose to navigate it is up to you. But carrying hatred for that part of yourself is a heavy burden. It might be less about the community and more about the fear of being alone, or the pain of not being seen as you want to be seen by someone you care about. There is no one way to be gay. You get to define what it means for you.

**3. The Home Environment**
Fighting with a parent who struggles with drinking, and having an absent dad, creates a lonely, unstable foundation. It’s no wonder you’re seeking escape. You deserve support and safety at home, and it’s heartbreaking that you’re not getting it consistently.

**4. The Coping Mechanisms**
Using weed and alcohol to numb the pain is understandable—you’re trying to survive. But these are temporary escapes, and they can deepen the loneliness over time. The fact that you’re reaching out here tells me part of you wants a way out of the cycle, not just an escape from the pain.

**5. The Suicidal Thoughts**
You’ve tried to end your life five times. That is a clear signal that the pain has become too much to carry alone. Telling your mom after the overdose was a brave and necessary step, even if it didn’t lead to the support you needed. This cannot be ignored.

---

### **What You Can Do Now—Concrete Steps**

**1. Immediate Safety**
If you feel suicidal again, please reach out to a crisis line immediately. They are there to listen, 24/7, without judgment.
- **Trevor Project (for LGBTQ+ youth):** 1-866-488-7386 or text “START” to 678-678
- **Crisis Text Line:** Text “HOME” to 741741
- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline:** 988

**2. Find One Trusted Adult**
Your mom may be struggling, but is there another adult—a coach, a teacher, a school counselor, a relative—you could talk to? You need someone in your corner who can help you access support. A coach, especially, might understand your discipline and drive and could be an ally.

**3. Professional Help**
The sleep deprivation, eating struggles, suicidal attempts, and substance use are signs of severe depression and trauma. This is **not** something you should have to handle alone. A therapist or counselor who specializes in LGBTQ+ youth or adolescent mental health could be a lifeline. If you’re worried about cost or privacy, a school counselor can often help connect you to resources.

**4. Small Steps Toward Self-Care**
- **Sleep and food:** Even if it’s small—a granola bar, a glass of milk, a few hours of rest—prioritize this. Your body is under immense stress.
- **Alternative escapes:** Instead of weed/alcohol, could you try something physical (like a run, lifting weights, even punching a pillow) or creative (writing, drawing, music) to release the pain? You’re an athlete—channeling emotion into physical exertion might feel more productive.
- **Connection:** You mentioned YouTube. Are there online communities (not necessarily LGBTQ-focused) around hobbies you enjoy? Gaming, rowing forums, comedy? Sometimes low-pressure interaction can help ease loneliness.

**5. Redefine “Future”**
You’re 15. The idea of being “alone for the rest of your life” is a feeling, not a fact. Right now, your world feels small and painful—but it will expand. There are people out there who will love you for exactly who you are, and you *will* meet them. But first, you need to get through this chapter. Survival is the priority.

---

You are in a storm right now. The wind is howling, the waves are crashing, and it feels endless. But storms pass. You have already survived 100% of your worst days. That is not a small thing.

You matter—not for your rowing achievements, not for making people laugh, but simply because you exist. Your feelings are valid. Your pain is real. And your future can be different from this present.

Please, take it one hour at a time. Reach out to one of those crisis lines tonight if you need to hear a human voice. You don’t have to do this alone.

I’m truly glad you wrote. Keep reaching out.

inputs: need advice.
My therapist invited me over for dinner and I don't know what to say
answer:
This is a significant boundary crossing in the therapeutic relationship, and your discomfort is completely valid and important to listen to.

Here is a breakdown of the situation and your options:

### **First, Understand This Is Not Standard or Ethical**
A therapist inviting a client to their home for a social dinner is a major red flag. The ethical foundation of therapy is built on **clear, professional boundaries**. This protects you, the client, and ensures the therapy space is solely for your healing. Socializing blurs those lines, can create confusion, dependency, and potentially exploit the power imbalance in the relationship.

Your gut feeling of "I don't know what to say" is your internal alarm system going off. Trust it.

### **Why This Is Problematic:**
*   **Dual Relationship:** It changes your relationship from professional to personal/social.
*   **Power Imbalance:** You may feel pressured to say "yes" to please your therapist, even if you're uncomfortable.
*   **Confidentiality:** Being in their personal space compromises the safety and neutrality of the therapeutic environment.
*   **It Stops Being About You:** Therapy time becomes about a social relationship, not your focused healing.

### **What You Can Say (Choose What Feels Safest)**

Your priority is to protect your own comfort and therapeutic process. You do **not** need to explain or justify upholding professional boundaries.

**Option 1: The Simple, Direct Decline (Recommended)**
You can respond via text or email, or state it at your next session.
*   *"Thank you for the invitation, but I need to keep our relationship strictly professional. I'd like to continue our work together as client and therapist."*
*   *"I appreciate the offer, but I don't think mixing social and professional relationships is right for me in therapy."*
*   *"No, thank you. I'm not comfortable with that."* (You never owe more explanation than this.)

**Option 2: If You Want to Address It Therapeutically**
You can use this as material *for* therapy, but only if you feel safe enough to do so.
*   At your next session: *"I wanted to talk about your dinner invitation. It made me uncomfortable and confused about our boundaries. Can we discuss what that was about and how to keep our work here clear?"*
*   This approach is powerful but requires a therapist who can handle the critique professionally. Their reaction will tell you everything you need to know.

**Option 3: If You Feel Too Uncomfortable to Continue**
If this invitation has damaged your trust, you have every right to terminate.
*   *"After your invitation to dinner, I no longer feel this is a professional therapeutic environment. I will be ending our sessions. Please send me a referral to another therapist."*

### **What to Do Next:**
1.  **Do Not Go to the Dinner.** Accepting normalizes this boundary violation.
2.  **Pause and Reflect.** How do you *feel* about your therapist now? Has trust been broken? Do you still feel safe?
3.  **Consider Reporting.** If this made you feel deeply uncomfortable, manipulated, or if the therapist persists, you can report them to their state licensing board. This is a serious ethical breach.
4.  **Find a New Therapist if Needed.** If you can't imagine rebuilding trust, it's perfectly reasonable to seek a new therapist. A good therapist will understand and might even help with a referral if you explain (briefly) that a boundary was crossed.

**Remember:** The therapy relationship is unique. It is intimate in terms of sharing your inner world, but it must remain professionally contained to be safe and effective. **A good therapist maintains these boundaries to protect you.**

You are not overreacting. Your uncertainty is a sign that a fundamental rule of the therapeutic frame has been broken. Please prioritize your own well-being.

inputs: My friend introduced me to his group of friends and I have bad anxiety - I went in for a handshake and this guy went in for some combo shit and our hands danced around each other for about 3 long seconds.
I cramp up thinking about it for days now.
answer:
First, take a deep breath.  
What you’re describing is one of those painfully awkward social moments that almost everyone has experienced in some form. It’s the “handshake vs. hug vs. fist bump” mismatch — a classic, cringe-inducing moment that feels ten times worse when you have anxiety.

Here are a few things to keep in mind:

1. **It’s way more common than you think.**  
   Almost everyone has had a moment like this — hands fumbling, both people trying to adjust, ending in an awkward half-handshake-half-something-else. It’s a universal sign of two people trying to be polite and meet each other halfway, but misreading cues.

2. **No one is judging you as harshly as you’re judging yourself.**  
   Your friend’s friend probably forgot about it 30 seconds later. These moments stand out massively in our own minds because we replay them, but to others it’s just a tiny, forgettable blip.

3. **Anxiety magnifies the embarrassment, but it doesn’t reflect reality.**  
   Your brain is stuck on a loop saying, “That was so awkward, they must think I’m weird,” but in reality, they likely just saw it as a funny, slightly awkward introduction — if they even remember it.

4. **If you see him again, you can laugh it off.**  
   Next time you meet, you could even break the ice by smiling and saying, “Hey, last time we did that awkward hand dance — how about a solid handshake this time?” That turns an awkward memory into a moment of connection. Most people would appreciate the humor and honesty.

5. **Be kind to yourself.**  
   You were meeting new people, which is hard even without anxiety. You put yourself out there, tried to be polite, and a minor coordination mishap happened. That doesn’t mean you failed socially — it just means you’re human.

Try to redirect your mental energy when you start cramping up about it. Remind yourself:  
- It’s over.  
- It was a tiny moment.  
- It doesn’t define you or the potential for future interactions.  
- Everyone has awkward moments; this was yours this time.

You’re okay. This will fade with time, especially if you meet them again and create new, better interactions.

inputs: Cat purring I found an app that allows you to listen to cats purring for free and for as long as you want.
Great for people who can't actually have a cat but still want the comfort of cat purring!
Just look up cat purring in the app store.
Thought it was neat and needed to share that this was a thing.
answer:
That's a fantastic find! It's a wonderful example of how technology can provide simple, therapeutic comfort. The sound of a cat purring is genuinely powerful for a lot of people.

**Why it's such a great idea:**

*   **Science-Backed Comfort:** Studies suggest the frequency of a cat's purr (between 25 and 150 Hz) may have therapeutic benefits, like lowering stress, reducing blood pressure, and even promoting bone and tissue healing. Whether it's the frequency or the deep psychological association with a content animal, the effect is real for many.
*   **Accessibility:** For people with allergies, housing restrictions, financial constraints, or lifestyles that can't accommodate a pet, this is a perfect solution.
*   **Focus and Sleep Aid:** The consistent, rhythmic sound can be excellent white noise for concentration, meditation, or falling asleep.
*   **Grief Support:** For someone who has recently lost a beloved pet, the familiar sound can be deeply comforting.

**For anyone looking, here are some common search terms that might help (besides just "cat purring"):**

*   Cat purr therapy
*   Calming cat sounds
*   Pet therapy sounds
*   White noise cat purr

Thanks for sharing this neat discovery. It's a small but meaningful tool for mental well-being, and it's great that you passed it on. A purr, even a digital one, can be a little gift of peace in a busy day

inputs: I hate food and my body I eat and I look and feel fucking disgusting.
I had a delicious burger and 3 onion rings and 2 beers and I feel so awful.
My gut is sticking out, I look fucking pregnant.
So disgusting.
I fucking hate myself.
I hate food.
I hate eating.
I hate my body.
I hate the way I look.
I hate the way I feel.
I recognize so many people look wayyyyyy worse than me.
But that doesn't help.
I wish we didn't have to eat.
I love tasting food, but I hate the way it makes me feel, both physically and emotionally.
Can't I just get an IV?
answer:
This sounds incredibly painful, and I'm so sorry you're in this much distress right now. The intensity of your feelings—the self-loathing, the disgust, the conflict—is a clear signal that you're suffering. Please know that what you're describing goes beyond a "bad body image day." It sounds like a very real and debilitating struggle with food and your body.

Let's break down what you've shared, because there are several important layers here:

**1. The Immediate Aftermath:** You ate a meal you found delicious (a burger, onion rings, beer). This triggered an intense physical sensation (bloating, "gut sticking out") and an even more intense **emotional and psychological reaction** (disgust, self-hatred, regret).

*   **Physically:** What you're describing—feeling extremely bloated and uncomfortable after a meal—can be due to many things: eating quickly, specific food sensitivities (like onions, gluten, carbonation), or simply the volume of food. It's a physical sensation, but it's being interpreted through a very painful mental filter.
*   **Emotionally:** The jump from physical discomfort to "I fucking hate myself" is the core of the issue. Food and your body have become tied to your entire sense of self-worth. This is a hallmark of eating disorders and severe disordered eating.

**2. The Core Conflict:** You've named it perfectly: *"I love tasting food, but I hate the way it makes me feel, both physically and emotionally."*
This is the brutal trap. The pleasure is instantly punished by pain. The thought of an IV is a desperate wish to escape this trap entirely—to get nourishment without the terror, guilt, and physical distress.

**3. The Logical Fallacy:** You mention knowing others "look way worse," but that it doesn't help. This is crucial. **This kind of suffering is not rational.** It's not about objective reality; it's about a broken relationship with food and your body. Telling yourself you "shouldn't" feel this way only adds a layer of shame on top of the existing pain.

**What This Sounds Like (And Why It Matters):**
This pattern of thinking and feeling is deeply aligned with conditions like:
*   **Orthorexia** (an obsession with "healthy" or "correct" eating that becomes damaging).
*   **Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD)** (a preoccupation with perceived flaws that others don't see).
*   **Other Specified Feeding or Eating Disorder (OSFED),** which includes patterns like purging disorder or night eating syndrome.
*   **Severe body image disturbance** that often co-occurs with anxiety and depression.

**The most important thing to understand is this: You are not broken. You are in pain. And this pain is treatable.**

**What You Can Do Right Now & Next:**

1.  **Crisis Intervention (If Needed):** If the thoughts of self-hatred become overwhelming or include thoughts of harming yourself, please reach out immediately. You can call or text the **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** or text "HOME" to **741741** to connect with the Crisis Text Line. You don't have to be suicidal to use these resources—they are for moments of extreme emotional pain.

2.  **Seek Professional Help: This is the most critical step.** You deserve support from people trained to help with this exact pain.
    *   **Start with your primary care doctor.** Be honest. You can read them what you wrote here. They can check for any underlying physical issues (like IBS, food intolerances) and provide referrals.
    *   **Seek out a therapist who specializes in eating disorders and body image.** Look for keywords like "HAES (Health at Every Size)," "intuitive eating," "body neutrality," and "eating disorder recovery."
    *   **Consider a registered dietitian (RD) who specializes in eating disorders.** Not a nutritionist, but a licensed RD. They can help you separate physical nourishment from emotional punishment and work on the physical discomfort you feel after eating.

3.  **Practice Immediate Self-Care (Not Punishment):**
    *   **Get away from the mirror.** The compulsion to check and criticize is part of the cycle.
    *   **Wear loose, comfortable clothing.** Be kind to your body in this moment of distress.
    *   **Do something gentle to distract and soothe your nervous system.** A warm (not hot) bath, a podcast, a simple video game, petting an animal—anything that engages a different part of your brain.
    *   **Try a self-compassion phrase,** even if you don't believe it. Something like: "This feeling is awful, and it's okay that I'm struggling. I am not my feelings. This is a moment of suffering."

4.  **Challenge the "IV" Thought:** The wish for an IV is a metaphor. It means you want the suffering to stop. You want to be nourished without the battle. Recovery is about finding a way to do that *with* food, by healing the relationship. It is possible.

You wrote this because a part of you knows this level of hatred is unsustainable and wants a way out. That part of you is strong and deserves to be listened to. This pain is your signal that something needs to change, not in your body's shape, but in your mind's relationship to it.

Please take this as seriously as you would a severe physical pain. You deserve care, you deserve to eat without terror, and you deserve to exist in your body without this constant war. The first step is telling a professional exactly what you told me. You can do this.

inputs: Dealing with setbacks after you've made progress?
Hey reddit friends.
I just moved across the country last month, away from my family and old friend group, because I missed the west coast and living by myself is cheaper out here.

Thing is, I ran out of my anti-anxiety meds (hydroxyzine/vistaril) last month, and getting back on track with them has not been easy (since Washington state healthcare seems to have more than a few dead ends when it comes to seeking treatment).
I've been feeling myself getting worse.
I'm frustrated with my inability to find work, how most of the doctors my healthcare points me to are either not accepting new patients or my healthcare.
It's hard to make progress when I'm feeling what little I had to hang on to slip away.
Do you guys have any suggestions for just.
Holding on when you feel completely alone and your few coping mechanisms are going to shit?
And even more of a longshot - anybody happen to have experience with eastern WA mental healthcare?
Is it this broken for everyone?
answer:
Hey. First, I want to acknowledge how incredibly difficult and overwhelming this situation is. You’ve taken a huge, brave step by moving across the country to build a life that feels right for you, and now you’re hitting systemic roadblocks at the exact moment your internal resources are depleted. That’s a brutal combination. Please know that what you’re feeling—the frustration, the sense of slipping backward—is a completely valid response to a genuinely hard situation. This isn’t a failure on your part; it’s a challenge you’re facing.

Let’s break this down into two parts: **Immediate Crisis Management** and **Building a Bridge to Stability.**

### 1. Immediate Crisis & Holding On Right Now

Your primary need is to stop the freefall. When meds run out and systems fail, you have to become your own first responder.

*   **Hydroxyzine Access - The Fastest Paths:**
    *   **Urgent Care or Walk-In Clinic:** This is your best bet for a short-term refill to bridge the gap. Take your old prescription bottle or a photo of it. Explain your situation clearly: "I recently moved, am out of my anxiety medication (hydroxyzine), and am hitting dead ends establishing new care. I need a bridge prescription to stabilize while I find a permanent provider." They do this often.
    *   **Telehealth Services:** Apps like **Teladoc, K Health, or Even Hims/Hers** can be useful for a one-time refill of a non-controlled medication like hydroxyzine. They operate in Washington. This might be the fastest way to get a script called into a local pharmacy.
    *   **Your Previous Prescriber:** Call your old doctor's office on the East Coast. Explain you've moved and are in a care gap. They might be willing to call in a **one-month emergency refill** to a pharmacy in WA. It's a common courtesy.

*   **Coping When Your Usual Coping Mechanisms Are Failing:**
    *   **Radical Acceptance & Self-Compassion:** Tell yourself, "This is a survival moment. My only job right now is to get through the next hour, the next day, with as little additional damage as possible." Lower the bar. A "good day" right now is one where you drink water, eat something, and do one tiny thing toward a solution.
    *   **Grounding for the Overwhelmed:** When anxiety peaks, try the **5-4-3-2-1 method** (name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste). Or simply hold an ice cube in your hand or splash very cold water on your face. It can shock the nervous system into a brief reset.
    *   **The "Do Nothing" Rest:** Literally lie on the floor. Set a timer for 15 minutes. Let the floor hold you. Your only task is to breathe. This isn't giving up; it's a strategic pause.
    *   **Body-Based Movement:** If you can, walk—not for exercise, but to change the physical sensation. Walk around the block and name the colors you see. Anxiety lives in the body; sometimes you have to move it through.

### 2. Navigating the Eastern WA Healthcare System (The Bridge)

Yes, it is often this broken, especially for mental health and especially in more rural or underserved areas. You are not crazy for feeling this way. The system is flawed. Your task is to become a persistent, strategic navigator of its flaws.

*   **Bypass the Dead-End Referrals:**
    *   **Call Your Insurance Company Directly.** Don't just use their online directory. Get a human on the line. Say: "I need a list of in-network psychiatrists or psychiatric nurse practitioners in [Your City/Zip] who are **verified as accepting new patients.** Please confirm their status while I am on the phone." Make them do the work.
    *   **Go Directly to Large Systems:** Look for major regional providers like **MultiCare, Providence, or Kaiser** (if you're with them). Even if they have waitlists, get on them. Their integrated systems sometimes have more leverage.
    *   **Consider a Primary Care Physician (PCP) First:** It is often **much faster** to get an appointment with a new PCP/Family Doctor. They can and often do manage prescriptions for hydroxyzine and other first-line anxiety meds while you wait for a psychiatry appointment. This is a critical stopgap.
    *   **Community Health Centers:** Look for **Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs)** in your area (like Yakima Neighborhood Health, CHAS Health in Spokane, etc.). They work on a sliding scale, cannot turn you away for inability to pay, and have integrated behavioral health. They are designed for this exact access problem.

*   **For the Job Search & Isolation:**
    *   **Tiny Wins:** Apply to one job, then stop for the day. That's a win. Update one section of your LinkedIn. That's a win. The goal is momentum, not volume.
    *   **Connection as Medicine:** You are not alone in feeling alone in a new place. Use **Meetup.com** or **Facebook groups** for hobbies (hiking, board games, book clubs) in your new city. Go with zero pressure to make a friend—just be around people. Libraries often have free community events.
    *   **Crisis & Support Lines:** **988** is the national Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. But also consider the **Washington Listens** support line (1-833-681-0211), which is specifically for stress related to transitions and isolation. They are there to talk, not just for imminent crisis.

### The Longshot Answer: Eastern WA Experience

Yes, it is notoriously difficult. Resources are concentrated west of the Cascades. Many people report long wait times, few specialists, and a lot of legwork. The strategies above—targeting community health centers, using a PCP as a bridge, and being a squeaky wheel with insurance—are the standard survival toolkit for the region. Persistence is your non-negotiable skill here.

**Final Thought:** You moved for a reason. You had the strength to make that change. This current setback is a brutal test of that strength, but it is not the end of your story. Right now, you are in the messy, painful middle chapter where the system is failing you. Your job is not to fix the system, but to deploy every short-term, scrappy workaround you can to get your feet back under you. Get the bridge prescription first. Then tackle one phone call tomorrow. You can do this. The progress you made isn't gone; it's just waiting for you to get through this storm.

inputs: god, i want you.
you don’t speak unless you have something to say.
and when you do—that voice.
so low, seductive.
haven’t stopped thinking about you today.
regaining consciousness in the middle of the night to feel your hand gently holding the back of my head.
how are you so good at talking me out of sex when i’m drunk like that?
then you just.
held me.
like you wanted to.
when/if this happens, i.
i just can’t stop thinking about it.
i want you.
answer:
This is raw and beautiful. It captures a specific, potent kind of longing—not just for physical touch, but for a particular *quality* of presence.

What you've written paints a portrait of a desire that is as much about safety and being truly *seen* as it is about passion. The power here lies in the contrasts:

*   **The voice** that is both silent and, when it speaks, "low, seductive." It holds authority.
*   **The act of being talked out of sex** when vulnerable, which becomes more intimate than the sex itself would have been. It speaks of care, of boundaries respected, of being protected from one's own less-inhibited self.
*   **The holding** that is active and intentional—"like you wanted to." It transforms a simple gesture into a profound gift.

The fragmented, breathless style—"then you just. / held me."—perfectly mirrors the way these memories hit: in flashes, in sensations, disarming in their simplicity and depth.

This isn't just "I want you." It's **"I want the version of me that exists in your quiet, holding presence. I want the peace and the wanting I feel there."**

It's a testament to how the most memorable intimacy often lives in these suspended moments: in the pause before the kiss, in the restraint that proves care, in the conscious touch in the dark. You've captured the ache for that specific, soul-quieting harbor in another person.

inputs: quit my job, moved out, started a new chapter.
more depressed than before.
i feel extremely lonely and depressed.
i thought this move would be a big step for me but i feel so depressed and i'm scared my depression will consume me as i am on my own now.
i don't know how im gonna find the energy to cook, to clean, to even live.
answer:
What you’re feeling is completely understandable, and it takes courage to admit it — especially when you’ve just taken a big step that was supposed to bring change and hope.  
It’s common for major life changes, even positive ones, to trigger a wave of loneliness and depression. The excitement of the “new chapter” can fade quickly when faced with the reality of being alone, without familiar routines or support systems.

Here are a few things to keep in mind:

1. **This doesn’t mean you made the wrong choice.**  
   Transitions are hard. Your mind and heart might be grieving the comfort of what was familiar, even if it wasn’t right for you. Depression can distort your perception, making it hard to see possibilities ahead.

2. **Be gentle with yourself about daily tasks.**  
   When you’re depressed, even small tasks feel enormous.  
   - Try to break things down into tiny steps: “I’ll just put one dish in the sink” or “I’ll just boil water for tea.”  
   - Use disposable plates for now if washing dishes feels impossible.  
   - Consider easy, minimal-effort meals (prepared foods, healthy snacks, smoothies) until you have more energy.

3. **Reach out for connection, even if it feels hard.**  
   Loneliness feeds depression.  
   - Can you schedule a regular call with a friend or family member, even just for 10 minutes?  
   - Look for low-pressure ways to be around people: a coffee shop, a library, a volunteer opportunity, or a class/group related to a mild interest.  
   - Sometimes online communities (supportive ones) can help bridge the gap when in-person feels too daunting.

4. **Consider professional support.**  
   If you’re feeling consumed by depression, this is a sign to seek help.  
   - A therapist can provide tools to navigate this transition and treat the depression.  
   - If therapy feels like too much right now, you could start by talking to your primary care doctor.  
   - In many places, there are crisis or warm lines (like 988 in the US) if you need to talk to someone immediately.

5. **Create a minimal structure for your day.**  
   When everything feels shapeless, depression worsens.  
   - Write down one small thing to do each day (shower, walk around the block, text one person).  
   - Try to get daylight and move your body gently — even sitting outside for a few minutes can sometimes shift things slightly.

6. **Remember why you made this change.**  
   Write down — even if just a few words — what you were hoping to find or leave behind when you quit your job and moved. It doesn’t have to be achieved yet, but reconnecting with your original hope might help you see this period as a painful but possibly necessary part of the journey.

You are not failing. You’re in the difficult middle part, where the old life is gone and the new one hasn’t yet taken shape.  
Depression makes it hard to believe things can change, but with support and small steps, this intensity of feeling can lessen.

You took a brave step. Now, the bravest thing might be to ask for help while you find your footing. You don’t have to do this alone, even if you’re physically on your own.

inputs: Would you call this an anxiety attack?
Last night, somebody said some things that were pretty hurtful and disparaging.
So I started feeling pretty strange.
I got really hot, and my heart started beating harder.
Not much faster, just harder.
My breathing didn't get faster, but it became really, like… I had to completely exhale before I could try to inhale again.
So after a while, that stuff died down, but I felt somewhat weak and lightheaded.
I wanted to lie down, which was great because it was bedtime anyway, except I also felt kinda tense, and kept pacing around in my room.
This isn't the first time this has happened.
But it's usually accompanied by extreme anger.
One time, my brother threatened to hit me so hard I'd be on the floor crying.
He didn't, but afterwards, I basically felt the way I just described, except I was also really angry.
Anyway, I don't really know if this is an anxiety attack, because when you read about that stuff, it sounds much worse than this.
Additionally, despite feeling all those things, my hands were relatively still.
I wasn't shaky or anything.
But I still felt like my body was… not following orders, breaking procedure, etc.
Like, what happened wasn't even that bad.
I had no reason to feel that way.
Also worth noting, is I tend to get like that whenever I disagree with someone, or vice versa.
Even online, which is actually where that thing happened.
(I'm posting this on an alt, because I'm pretty sure the people who know my main account would put two and two together, and they'd know what set me off.
answer:
Thank you for sharing this with such detail. It takes a lot of self-awareness to notice and describe these physical and emotional shifts so clearly.

Based on what you've described, **yes, this sounds very much like an anxiety attack, which can also be called a panic attack.** It's important to know that the "textbook" version you read about is often the most extreme, dramatic presentation. Attacks exist on a spectrum, and what you experienced is a very valid and common form.

Let's break down why your experience aligns with this:

*   **The Trigger:** A socially painful, hurtful event (online or in-person) is a classic trigger for anxiety, especially if it involves conflict, criticism, or threat.
*   **The Physical Symptoms:**
    *   **Heat & Heartbeat:** Feeling hot and having a pounding heart (even if not faster) are hallmark signs of your sympathetic nervous system activating (the "fight-or-flight" response).
    *   **Breathing Pattern:** Your description of breathing is key. That need to fully exhale before inhaling again is a form of dysregulated breathing often seen in anxiety. It can lead to lightheadedness and weakness.
    *   **Weakness & Lightheadedness:** These can result from that altered breathing and the surge of adrenaline.
    *   **Tension & Pacing:** The "motor restlessness"—needing to pace despite feeling weak—is your body's trapped "fight-or-flight" energy with nowhere to go. It's a powerful urge to *do something*.
*   **The Emotional Component:** You mention this often comes with extreme anger. This is incredibly important. For many people (especially, but not exclusively, men and those socialized to suppress "vulnerable" emotions like fear or sadness), **anxiety manifests as anger or rage.** The threat system is activated, and anger is the "fight" part of the response. Your brother's threat is a perfect example—your body was preparing to defend itself.
*   **The Cognitive Dissonance:** "What happened wasn't even that bad. I had no reason to feel that way." This is one of the most telling signs of an anxiety disorder. Your logical mind is downplaying the threat, but your nervous system is reacting as if it's real and present. This mismatch is deeply confusing and distressing.
*   **The Context:** That this happens during disagreements—even minor online ones—points to a heightened sensitivity to conflict, rejection, or invalidation. Your nervous system may be primed to perceive these as threats.

**A few important clarifications:**

1.  **Not Shaky:** Attacks don't *require* visible shaking. Internal tremors, pounding, and tension are just as valid.
2.  **"Body Not Following Orders":** This is an excellent way to describe the autonomic nature of an attack. You're not in conscious control of your heartbeat, temperature, or breathing patterns during this—that's the job of the autonomic nervous system, which has been triggered into overdrive.
3.  **The Online Component:** The fact that an online interaction can trigger this is very common. Our brains don't fully distinguish the threat source; perceived social danger activates the same pathways.

**What you can consider doing:**

*   **Validate Your Experience:** The first step is to stop minimizing it. Your body was having a real, physiological reaction to perceived threat. It wasn't an overreaction; it was a *nervous system* reaction.
*   **Grounding Techniques for the Physical Symptoms:** When you feel the heat and pounding start, try:
    *   **Paced Breathing:** Inhale slowly for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale slowly for 6. This directly counters the "need to exhale" pattern and regulates your system.
    *   **Temperature Change:** Splash cold water on your face, hold an ice cube, or step outside for cool air. This can shock your system back to equilibrium.
    *   **Focus on Senses:** Name 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
*   **Address the Anger-Anxiety Link:** When you feel the anger surge, ask yourself: "What is the threat here? Am I feeling scared, hurt, or invalidated?" Naming the underlying vulnerability can sometimes take power away from the anger.
*   **Consider Professional Insight:** Speaking to a therapist or counselor could be incredibly helpful. They can help you:
    *   Understand why your nervous system is so reactive to conflict/disagreement.
    *   Develop strategies to manage the physical symptoms.
    *   Untangle the anger from the anxiety and build new response pathways.
    *   This isn't about something being "wrong" with you; it's about gaining tools for a system that is currently working against you.

You've shown great courage in posting this, especially on an alt to protect your privacy. What you're describing is a real, recognizable pattern of anxiety. Recognizing it for what it is—a nervous system response, not a character flaw or overreaction—is the most powerful first step you can take.

inputs: Surprise guest visit.
One ativan already down.
Gonna be an interesting night
answer:
It sounds like you're preparing for a challenging situation. I'm concerned about your mention of taking Ativan, especially if you're anticipating a stressful event.

**A few important things to consider:**

1.  **Medication Safety:** Ativan (lorazepam) is a powerful medication. It's crucial to only take it as prescribed by your doctor. Taking more than prescribed, or mixing it with alcohol or other substances, can be dangerous and lead to excessive sedation, respiratory depression, or other serious side effects.
2.  **Your Well-being:** "Gonna be an interesting night" suggests you're expecting difficulty. Please prioritize your own mental and emotional state. It's okay to set boundaries, even with a surprise guest.
3.  **Support:** Do you have someone you can check in with tonight, either in person or by phone, who knows what's going on and can be a support?

**If you start feeling overly sedated, confused, or have trouble breathing, please seek medical attention immediately.**

I'm here if you want to talk about what you're facing or need help thinking through strategies to get through the visit. Your safety is the most important thing.

inputs: I need to hear someone yell at me about my ghosting habit.
answer:
**GET OVER HERE AND LISTEN UP.**

This isn’t a gentle nudge. This is a full-volume, in-your-face reality check.

You are treating people like disposable tissues. You connect, you chat, you make plans, you make someone feel seen… and then you just vanish. Poof. Into the digital ether. You think that’s just being “busy” or “avoiding awkwardness”? **NO.** It’s cowardly. It’s disrespectful. And it’s deeply damaging.

Do you have any idea what’s on the other side of your silence?

*   **A person refreshing their phone, wondering what they did wrong.**
*   **Someone’s self-esteem taking a direct hit because your silence screams, “You are not worth a single sentence of explanation.”**
*   **A friend who trusted you, now questioning every interaction you ever had.**
*   **You are teaching people that connection is meaningless and that they shouldn’t bother being vulnerable.**

You’re stealing their closure to buy yourself a moment of comfort. That’s selfish. Period.

You’re also wrecking your own reputation. People talk. They remember. You’re building a name for yourself as unreliable, emotionally immature, and untrustworthy. Opportunities—friendships, relationships, even professional connections—will dry up because people will learn you’re a vanishing act.

**YOU ARE NOT A GHOST.** You are a living, breathing human being with the capacity for basic decency. That decency requires a minimum of 10 seconds of bravery.

**“Hey, I’ve got a lot on my plate and can’t keep up this chat. All the best.”**
**“I don’t think we’re a match romantically, but I wish you well.”**
**“I need to step back from this friendship for a while. It’s not you, it’s where I’m at.”**

See? Not a novel. Not therapy. Just a clear, kind, HUMAN ending.

Stop hiding. Stop rationalizing. Your habit is a choice, and it’s a shitty one. **PUT ON YOUR BIG-KID PANTS AND COMMUNICATE.** End things with clarity, not with silence. The temporary discomfort of sending a text is NOTHING compared to the lingering damage you inflict by disappearing.

**DO BETTER. START TODAY.** The next time you feel the urge to ghost, hear this yell in your head. Then open your keyboard and type a single, merciful sentence of closure. That’s all it takes to stop being part of the problem.

Now go fix it.

inputs: I can't stop thinking about my ex-boyfriend.
It's been months since we broke up.
answer:
It’s completely normal to still think about your ex months after a breakup — healing isn’t linear, and it doesn’t have a set timeline.  
The fact that it’s on your mind likely means there are unresolved feelings or lessons your heart and mind are still processing.

Here are a few gentle suggestions that might help:

1. **Allow yourself to feel** — It’s okay to grieve the relationship and miss what you had. Suppressing emotions often makes them stick around longer.

2. **Reflect on “why” it ended** — Sometimes our minds romanticize the past. Writing down the reasons you’re no longer together can help balance the nostalgia with reality.

3. **Create space for new experiences** — When we’re stuck in memories, sometimes it’s because our present feels empty. Try a new hobby, reconnect with friends, or set a personal goal — even small changes can shift your focus.

4. **Limit reminders** — If you’re still checking his social media or keeping mementos in sight, consider a temporary break from those triggers.

5. **Talk it out** — Sometimes sharing with a trusted friend or a therapist can help you process things more fully.

6. **Practice self-compassion** — Be kind to yourself. Healing takes time, and it’s okay if you’re not “over it” yet.

If you feel truly stuck or the thoughts are interfering with your daily life, speaking with a counselor could provide more tailored support.

You’re not alone in this — many people go through this phase after a meaningful relationship ends. Be patient with your heart.

inputs: I think I might have an eating disorder.
answer:
It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge that something might be wrong, and reaching out for clarity is a significant first step. I hear you, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid.

Since I'm an AI, I can't diagnose you, but I can offer some guidance and support.

**What you can do right now:**

1.  **Reach out to a professional.** This is the most important step. Talking to a doctor, a therapist, or a registered dietitian who specializes in eating disorders can provide you with a proper assessment and a safe, confidential space to explore your concerns. They are trained to help without judgment.
    *   You can start with your primary care physician.
    *   Look for therapists specializing in **eating disorders, disordered eating, or body image**.

2.  **Contact a helpline.** These are free, confidential, and staffed by trained individuals who understand. You don't have to be in crisis to call.
    *   **National Eating Disorders Association (NEDA) Helpline:** You can call, text, or chat online at [www.nationaleatingdisorders.org](https://www.nationaleatingdisorders.org) or call 1-800-931-2237.
    *   **Beat Eating Disorders (UK):** [www.beateatingdisorders.org.uk](https://www.beateatingdisorders.org.uk)

3.  **Be gentle with yourself.** Thoughts and behaviors around food and body image are often rooted in deeper emotions and are not a sign of weakness or failure. Self-compassion is crucial right now.

**Some signs that it's important to seek help include:**
*   Preoccupation with weight, food, calories, and dieting.
*   Feeling extreme guilt or anxiety around eating.
*   Avoiding social situations involving food.
*   Feeling a loss of control over eating (e.g., binge eating).
*   Compensatory behaviors (like vomiting, excessive exercise, or laxative use).
*   Your self-worth being heavily influenced by body shape and weight.
*   Significant changes in weight (up or down).

**Remember:** Eating disorders are complex mental health conditions, but they are also **treatable**. Recovery is possible, and it starts exactly where you are now—by questioning and seeking support.

You are not alone in this. Many people have walked this path and found their way to healing with the right help. Taking this first step of acknowledgment is a powerful act of self-care.

Is there a specific aspect you're feeling most concerned about, or would you like help finding resources in your area? I'm here to support you in navigating the next steps.

inputs: I'm having trouble with my body image and feeling pressure to look a certain way.
answer:
It’s completely normal to feel this way, especially with the constant exposure to idealized images in media and social platforms. Many people struggle with body image, and it’s important to remember that your worth isn’t tied to your appearance.

Here are a few things that might help:

1. **Limit exposure to unrealistic standards** — Consider curating your social media feeds to include body-positive accounts, diverse representations, and content that focuses on health and well-being rather than appearance.

2. **Practice self-compassion** — Try to speak to yourself as you would to a friend. Notice and challenge negative self-talk when it arises.

3. **Focus on what your body can do** — Appreciating your body for its strength, resilience, and ability to experience life can help shift focus away from just looks.

4. **Connect with supportive people** — Surround yourself with those who value you for who you are, not how you look.

5. **Consider professional support** — If these feelings are significantly affecting your life, a therapist or counselor trained in body image issues can offer valuable guidance.

Remember, you are more than your appearance — your thoughts, feelings, talents, and relationships make you uniquely you. It’s okay to have difficult days, but be gentle with yourself as you work toward a healthier self-view.

inputs: I have trouble sleeping at night and it's affecting my daily life.
What can I do?
answer:
I'm sorry to hear you're struggling with sleep — it's incredibly frustrating when it affects your daily life. You're not alone, and there are many effective strategies to improve sleep. Let's break this down into actionable steps.

### Immediate Steps to Try Tonight
1.  **Establish a "Wind-Down" Routine:** Start 60 minutes before bed. Dim lights, turn off screens (phones, TV, computers), and do something calming like reading a physical book, listening to soft music, or taking a warm bath/shower (the drop in body temperature afterward signals sleep).
2.  **Optimize Your Sleep Environment:**
    *   **Dark:** Use blackout curtains or an eye mask.
    *   **Cool:** Keep the room between 60-67°F (15-19°C).
    *   **Quiet:** Use earplugs or a white noise machine.
    *   **Comfortable:** Ensure your mattress and pillows are supportive.
3.  **Mind the Clock:**
    *   **Be Consistent:** Go to bed and wake up at the same time every day, even on weekends.
    *   **If You Can't Sleep:** If you're awake in bed for more than 20-30 minutes, get up. Go to another dimly lit room and do something quiet (like reading) until you feel sleepy. This helps break the association between bed and anxiety/alertness.
4.  **Watch Your Intake:**
    *   Avoid caffeine (coffee, tea, soda, chocolate) after 2 PM.
    *   Avoid heavy meals, alcohol, and nicotine close to bedtime. Alcohol may help you fall asleep but severely disrupts sleep quality later in the night.

### Lifestyle Adjustments for the Day
1.  **Get Morning Light:** Exposure to natural sunlight within 30-60 minutes of waking helps regulate your circadian rhythm (sleep-wake cycle).
2.  **Exercise Regularly:** Aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate exercise most days, but finish intense workouts at least 3 hours before bed.
3.  **Manage Stress & Worry:**
    *   Keep a "worry journal" in the evening. Write down what's on your mind and set it aside for tomorrow.
    *   Practice relaxation techniques like **deep breathing (4-7-8 method)**, progressive muscle relaxation, or a short guided meditation before bed (apps like Calm or Insight Timer can help).

### When to Seek Professional Help
If you've tried these strategies consistently for 2-3 weeks and see no improvement, **it's crucial to consult a doctor.** Chronic insomnia can be a symptom of other conditions (e.g., sleep apnea, anxiety, thyroid issues) or a disorder itself. A professional can provide:
*   **Accurate Diagnosis:** To rule out underlying causes.
*   **Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I):** This is the gold-standard, first-line treatment for chronic insomnia. It's more effective and sustainable than sleep medication in the long run. It helps you change thoughts and behaviors around sleep.
*   **Short-term Medication Options:** If appropriate, a doctor can discuss these, but they are typically not a long-term solution on their own.

### Key Takeaway
Start with one or two changes from the "Immediate Steps" tonight. The most powerful levers are often **consistency (schedule), light exposure (morning sun), and breaking the anxiety cycle** around sleep. Be patient with yourself—it can take time to reset your sleep patterns.

You've taken the first important step by seeking solutions. I hope you find relief soon.

inputs: I'm having trouble in my relationship and I don't know what to do.
answer:
It sounds like you're going through a difficult time, and it's completely understandable to feel stuck or unsure. Relationship challenges are very common, but they can feel overwhelming when you're in the middle of them.

To offer more specific guidance, could you share a little more about what's troubling you? For example:

*   Is it a specific conflict, a feeling of distance, a breach of trust, or something else?
*   How long have you been feeling this way?
*   Have you tried to talk about it with your partner?

In the meantime, here are a few general steps that often help people gain clarity:

1.  **Reflect for Yourself First:** Try to identify the core issue. Is it about communication, unmet needs, values, respect, or something else? Writing your thoughts down can help untangle them.
2.  **Choose a Calm Time to Talk:** If you feel safe to do so, approach your partner for a conversation. Use "I feel" statements (e.g., "I feel hurt when...") instead of "You always" statements, which can sound accusatory.
3.  **Listen Actively:** If you have a conversation, try to listen to understand their perspective, not just to prepare your next point. Sometimes the real issue isn't the first thing mentioned.
4.  **Consider Professional Help:** A couples therapist or counselor is a neutral third party who can provide tools and a safe space for communication. It's a sign of strength, not weakness, to seek help.
5.  **Prioritize Your Well-being:** Spend time on your own hobbies, friends, and self-care. This helps you maintain your balance and think more clearly.

**Please remember:** If your relationship involves **abuse of any kind (emotional, verbal, physical)**, or if you feel unsafe, the most important step is to prioritize your safety. In that case, please consider reaching out to a trusted friend, family member, or a domestic violence hotline for confidential support.

You don't have to figure this out alone. Whatever you're facing, there are resources and people who can help. I'm here to listen if you'd like to share more.

inputs: I'm having trouble with my self-esteem.
I don't feel good about myself.
answer:
I'm really sorry to hear you're feeling this way. It's a difficult and painful experience, but it's also incredibly common. The fact that you're acknowledging it is a significant first step toward feeling better.

Let's break this down and talk about some things that might help.

### First, Acknowledge and Validate
It's okay to not feel okay. You don't have to beat yourself up for having low self-esteem. Think of this feeling as a signal, like a check-engine light on a car. It's telling you something needs attention, not that the car is broken beyond repair.

### Common Roots of Low Self-Esteem
Understanding where these feelings might come from can help separate *you* from the *feeling*. Common sources include:
*   **The Inner Critic:** That voice that says "You're not good enough," "You messed up again," or compares you unfairly to others.
*   **Past Experiences:** Criticism, bullying, neglect, or trauma from childhood or past relationships can leave deep scars.
*   **Unrealistic Standards:** Holding yourself to impossible ideals (from social media, family, culture) that no one could realistically meet.
*   **Focusing on Perceived Flaws:** Magnifying your weaknesses and discounting your strengths.
*   **All-or-Nothing Thinking:** Seeing a single mistake as proof of total failure.

### Practical Steps You Can Take (Start Small)

1.  **Practice Self-Compassion.** Talk to yourself like you would talk to a dear friend who was hurting. If your friend said, "I feel worthless," you wouldn't agree with them. You'd list their good qualities and offer kindness. Try to extend that same kindness to yourself. A simple start: When you make a mistake, instead of "I'm so stupid," try "I'm human, and I'm learning."

2.  **Challenge the Inner Critic.** When a negative thought pops up ("I'm a failure"), don't just accept it. **Ask for evidence.** "What proof do I have that I'm a *total* failure?" Then, ask for counter-evidence. "What are some things I have done okay or well, even recently?" This isn't about fake positivity; it's about fairness.

3.  **Use "The Fact vs. The Feeling" Tool.** Separate the objective fact from the subjective feeling.
    *   **Feeling:** "I am incompetent."
    *   **Fact:** "I am struggling with a specific task right now, and I feel frustrated." This makes the problem smaller and more manageable.

4.  **Do Things That Build "Evidence."** Self-esteem is built on a foundation of small accomplishments and self-respect.
    *   **Set a Tiny, Achievable Goal:** Make your bed. Cook one healthy meal. Take a 10-minute walk. Completing it gives you a small win.
    *   **Practice a Skill:** Improving at something—anything (drawing, a sport, a video game, cooking)—builds a sense of competence.
    *   **Help Someone:** Hold a door, give a genuine compliment, volunteer. This shifts focus outward and reminds you of your value to others.

5.  **Curate Your Inputs.**
    *   **Social Media:** Unfollow accounts that make you feel inadequate. Follow accounts that inspire, educate, or make you laugh.
    *   **People:** Spend more time with people who uplift you and less with those who drain you or are critical.
    *   **Self-Talk:** Pay attention to your internal dialogue. Would you let someone else talk to you that way?

6.  **Take Care of the Basics.** It sounds simple, but it's powerful. When we feel bad, we often neglect these, which makes us feel worse.
    *   **Sleep:** Prioritize it.
    *   **Nutrition:** Feed your body decent food.
    *   **Movement:** Even gentle stretching can improve your mental state.
    *   **Sunlight:** Get outside for a few minutes a day.

### When to Seek Professional Help
**Please consider this strongly.** A therapist or counselor is trained to help you:
*   Unpack the deeper roots of your low self-esteem.
*   Develop concrete cognitive and behavioral tools to change your thought patterns.
*   Work through past trauma in a safe space.
Thinking of therapy as a sign of weakness is like thinking a personal trainer is for weak people. It's a tool for building strength with expert guidance.

**You are not alone in this.** The way you feel about yourself right now is not a fixed, permanent truth. It's a state that can be changed with patience, practice, and often, a little help.

Be patient with yourself. Healing isn't linear. Some days will be better than others. The goal isn't to never have a negative thought again, but to change your relationship with those thoughts so they don't control you.

You reached out here, which shows strength and a desire to feel better. That's a powerful starting point. Be kind to yourself today.

inputs: I'm struggling with addiction and it's affecting my life in many negative ways.
answer:
Thank you for sharing that with me. It takes courage to acknowledge that addiction is impacting your life, and that’s an important first step.

What you’re going through is incredibly difficult, but **you are not alone**, and recovery is possible. Many people have walked this path and found their way to a healthier, more fulfilling life.

Here are some immediate steps you might consider:

### 1. **Reach Out for Professional Help**
   - **Talk to a doctor or addiction specialist.** They can provide a safe, non-judgmental assessment and discuss treatment options, which may include therapy, medication, or detox programs.
   - **Consider therapy.** A therapist trained in addiction can help you understand the root causes, develop coping strategies, and build a recovery plan.

### 2. **Connect with Support Groups**
   - Groups like **Alcoholics Anonymous (AA), Narcotics Anonymous (NA), or SMART Recovery** offer community, shared experiences, and structured support. Hearing from others who understand can reduce feelings of isolation.

### 3. **Lean on Trusted People**
   - Confide in someone you trust—a friend, family member, or mentor. Having even one person who knows what you’re facing can make a big difference.

### 4. **Prioritize Your Safety**
   - If you’re experiencing severe withdrawal symptoms (e.g., shaking, severe anxiety, hallucinations), seek medical attention immediately. Some withdrawals can be dangerous and require professional supervision.

### 5. **Small Steps Forward**
   - Recovery is often a process, not a single event. Be patient with yourself. Even acknowledging the problem and researching help is a step in the right direction.

### 6. **Crisis Resources (Available 24/7)**
   - **SAMHSA National Helpline:** 1-800-662-HELP (4357) – Free, confidential treatment referral and information (U.S.).
   - **Crisis Text Line:** Text HOME to 741741 (U.S./Canada) to connect with a crisis counselor.
   - If you’re outside the U.S., many countries have similar helplines—I can help you find one if you share your location.

### Remember:
- **Addiction is a medical condition,** not a moral failing. It doesn’t define your worth.
- **Relapse can be part of the journey** for many—it doesn’t mean failure. What matters is continuing to move forward.
- **Self-compassion is crucial.** Replace shame with curiosity: “What is this addiction helping me cope with? What do I truly need?”

You’ve already started by reaching out here. Would you like help finding specific resources in your area, or do you have questions about what the next steps might look like? I’m here to listen and support you however I can.

inputs: I'm having trouble with my addiction and I don't know how to stop.
answer:
It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge that you're struggling with an addiction and want to stop. That's a significant first step, and you should be proud of yourself for reaching out.

Here’s a roadmap to help you begin. Remember, recovery is a process, not a single event, and it's okay to ask for help every step of the way.

### 1. **Acknowledge and Accept**
You've already started here. Be honest with yourself about the impact the addiction is having on your life—your health, relationships, work, and happiness. Acceptance removes denial, which is the biggest barrier to change.

### 2. **Reach Out for Professional Help**
You don't have to do this alone. Addiction is a complex condition that often requires support.
*   **Talk to a Doctor:** They can assess your health, discuss safe withdrawal options (if needed), and provide referrals.
*   **Find a Therapist or Counselor:** Look for someone specializing in addiction or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT). They can help you understand the root causes and develop coping strategies.
*   **Consider Treatment Programs:** Depending on the severity, outpatient programs, intensive outpatient programs (IOP), or residential treatment might be appropriate.

### 3. **Explore Support Groups**
Connecting with others who understand is incredibly powerful.
*   **12-Step Programs:** Groups like Alcoholics Anonymous (AA), Narcotics Anonymous (NA), or SMART Recovery offer structured peer support and a sense of community.
*   **Sharing with a Trusted Person:** Confide in a close friend or family member. Letting someone in can relieve the immense burden of secrecy.

### 4. **Identify Your Triggers**
What situations, emotions, people, or places make you crave the substance or behavior? Common triggers are stress, boredom, loneliness, or certain social settings. Once you identify them, you can start to develop a plan to avoid or manage them.

### 5. **Develop New Coping Skills**
Addiction often fills a void or manages pain. You need to build healthier tools.
*   **For Stress:** Try exercise, meditation, deep breathing, or yoga.
*   **For Boredom/Loneliness:** Pick up a new hobby, volunteer, or reconnect with old friends in healthy settings.
*   **For Urges:** Have a "delay" strategy. Tell yourself you'll wait 15 minutes, and during that time, distract yourself—go for a walk, call your support person, or play a game.

### 6. **Make Practical Changes**
*   **Remove Temptations:** Get rid of the substance or anything associated with it from your home.
*   **Change Routines:** Avoid the places or people linked to your addiction.
*   **Focus on Health:** Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and hydration. Physical well-being significantly impacts mental resilience.

### 7. **Be Kind to Yourself**
*   **Expect Setbacks:** Relapse can be a part of recovery for many. It doesn't mean you've failed. It means you need to adjust your plan and learn from the experience.
*   **Practice Self-Compassion:** Talk to yourself as you would a good friend. Shame and guilt are fuel for addiction; self-compassion is fuel for recovery.
*   **Celebrate Small Wins:** Every day, hour, or minute you choose not to engage in the addiction is a victory. Acknowledge your strength.

### **Immediate Action Steps You Can Take Today:**
1.  **Call a Helpline:** They are free, confidential, and available 24/7.
    *   **SAMHSA National Helpline (US):** 1-800-662-HELP (4357)
    *   **Crisis Text Line (US/Canada/UK/Ireland):** Text HOME to 741741
    *   Find a similar national helpline if you're in another country.
2.  **Make an Appointment:** Call your doctor or look up therapists/support groups in your area.
3.  **Tell One Person:** "I'm struggling and I need help." Say it out loud to someone you trust.

This is the beginning of taking your power back. The path isn't always straight, but every step forward is a step toward a life you control. You deserve health, peace, and freedom. Start with one phone call. **You can do this.**

inputs: I'm not sure if I should start taking medication for my anxiety.
answer:
Deciding whether to start medication for anxiety is a significant and personal choice. It’s completely normal to feel uncertain. Here are some considerations that might help you navigate this decision:

### 1. **Assess the Severity and Impact**
   - **How much is anxiety affecting your daily life?** Consider your work, relationships, sleep, and overall well-being. If anxiety is significantly impairing your functioning or causing persistent distress, medication might be worth discussing.
   - **Duration:** Has this been going on for weeks, months, or longer? Chronic anxiety often benefits from professional intervention.

### 2. **Explore Non-Medication Approaches First (or Alongside)**
   - **Therapy:** Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), mindfulness-based therapies, and other forms of counseling are evidence-based treatments for anxiety. Many people find therapy alone to be effective.
   - **Lifestyle changes:** Regular exercise, good sleep hygiene, reducing caffeine/alcohol, stress management techniques (e.g., meditation, yoga), and social support can all help reduce anxiety.
   - If you haven’t tried these yet, you might consider starting here, especially if your anxiety is mild to moderate.

### 3. **Consult a Professional**
   - **Talk to a doctor or psychiatrist.** They can provide a proper diagnosis, discuss the pros and cons of medication, and explain how it works. They’ll consider your specific symptoms, medical history, and any other conditions.
   - **Be open about your concerns.** Share your hesitations—fear of side effects, dependency, stigma, etc. A good doctor will listen and help you weigh the risks and benefits.

### 4. **Understand How Anxiety Medications Work**
   - **Types:** Common options include SSRIs, SNRIs, or occasionally short-term use of benzodiazepines. SSRIs/SNRIs are often first-line for long-term management but can take weeks to work.
   - **Purpose:** Medication isn’t a “cure” but can reduce symptoms to a level where you can more effectively engage in therapy and make lifestyle changes.
   - **Trial and adjustment:** Finding the right medication or dose can take time, and side effects are possible but often manageable.

### 5. **Consider a Combined Approach**
   - Many people benefit most from **medication + therapy**. Medication can ease symptoms enough to make therapy more effective, and therapy can provide long-term coping skills.

### 6. **Reflect on Your Personal Feelings**
   - **Stigma:** Some people feel that taking medication means they’re “weak” or “broken,” but anxiety is a medical condition—treating it is no different than treating diabetes or high blood pressure.
   - **Fear of dependency:** Discuss this with your doctor. Some anxiety medications (like certain antidepressants) are not addictive, while others (like benzodiazepines) require careful management.
   - **Your goals:** What do you hope treatment will help you achieve? Clarity here can guide your decision.

### 7. **You Can Re-evaluate**
   - Starting medication doesn’t have to be permanent. You can try it under medical supervision and, if it’s not helping or you wish to stop, work with your doctor to taper off safely.

### Next Steps:
- **Schedule an appointment** with a primary care doctor or psychiatrist to discuss your options.
- **Consider a therapist** for an evaluation and to explore non-medication strategies.
- **Give yourself permission** to prioritize your mental health—it’s a sign of strength to seek help.

Ultimately, the decision is yours, but it’s best made with professional guidance and accurate information. Whatever you choose, taking steps to address your anxiety is a positive move toward well-being.

inputs: I'm feeling really anxious lately, and I don't know how to cope with it.
answer:
I'm sorry to hear you're feeling anxious — that's a really tough place to be. It's completely normal to feel this way sometimes, and acknowledging it is a brave first step.

Here are a few gentle, evidence-based strategies you can try right now:

**For immediate relief:**
1. **Breathe slowly.** Try inhaling for 4 seconds, holding for 4, exhaling for 6. This helps calm your nervous system.
2. **Ground yourself.** Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, and 1 you can taste.
3. **Move your body.** A short walk, stretching, or shaking out your limbs can release tension.

**For the longer term:**
- **Limit caffeine and sugar**, especially if you're feeling jittery.
- **Write down** what’s making you anxious. Sometimes getting it out of your head helps.
- **Talk to someone** — a friend, family member, or therapist. You don’t have to carry this alone.

**When to seek help:**
If anxiety is interfering with your daily life, sleep, or relationships, consider speaking with a mental health professional. They can offer tools and strategies tailored to you.

You’re not alone in this, and it’s okay to ask for support. Would you like to talk more about what’s been coming up for you?

inputs: I'm having trouble with my identity and don't know who I am.
answer:
That sounds like a deeply unsettling and challenging experience. Feeling unsure about your identity is more common than you might think, and it can be a sign of growth, even though it's uncomfortable.

First, please know that it's okay to not have all the answers right now. Identity isn't always a fixed point; for many people, it's a journey of discovery that unfolds over a lifetime.

Here are a few gentle steps you might consider, not as a solution, but as a way to start exploring:

### 1. **Practice Self-Observation Without Judgment**
   *   Instead of asking "Who am I?" (which can feel overwhelming), try asking "What do I notice about myself today?"
   *   Pay attention to small things: What made you feel energized this week? What drained you? What values felt important in a recent decision (e.g., honesty, kindness, creativity, security)? These are clues.

### 2. **Separate "You" from External Expectations**
   *   Often, identity confusion comes from layers of expectations—from family, culture, work, or social media. Ask yourself: "Which parts of my life feel like *my* choice, and which parts feel like I 'should' be doing them?"
   *   It's okay to gently set aside the "shoulds" to see what remains.

### 3. **Explore Through Small Experiments**
   *   You don't need to make grand declarations. Try small, low-stakes experiments. Join a new club, try a different style of clothing, volunteer for a cause, or learn a simple new skill. Notice how these activities make you *feel*.
   *   Keep a journal of these experiences and your reactions to them.

### 4. **Connect with Stories**
   *   Read books, watch films, or listen to podcasts about people who have gone through similar transitions or explorations. Sometimes seeing another person's journey can provide language and validation for your own.

### 5. **Consider Professional Support**
   *   A therapist or counselor, especially one who specializes in identity issues or life transitions, can provide a safe, non-judgmental space to untangle these feelings. They can offer frameworks and tools that are difficult to access on our own.

### 6. **Embrace the "In-Between"**
   *   It's okay to be in a state of "becoming." You don't have to have a solid, finalized identity to be valid or worthy. Sometimes the most authentic growth happens in these uncertain phases.

**A crucial reminder:** If this feeling is accompanied by deep sadness, hopelessness, or a loss of sense of reality, please reach out to a mental health professional or a trusted person in your life immediately. You don't have to navigate this alone.

This search for self is a profound and courageous undertaking. Be patient and as kind to yourself as you would be to a close friend in the same situation. You are not broken for questioning; you are human, and this questioning can ultimately lead you to a more authentic and resilient sense of self.

inputs: I'm struggling with my identity and don't know who I am, what can I do?
answer:
That’s a deeply human and significant struggle — many people go through periods of questioning their identity, especially during times of transition, stress, or personal growth. It can be unsettling, but it’s also an opportunity to explore and understand yourself more deeply. Here are some steps you can consider:

---

### **1. Give yourself permission to not know**
It’s okay to feel uncertain. Identity isn’t always fixed — it can evolve over time. Be gentle with yourself and recognize that questioning is part of growth, not a failure.

---

### **2. Reflect on your values and beliefs**
Sometimes, who we are is rooted in what matters most to us. Ask yourself:
- What principles guide my decisions?
- When do I feel most proud of myself?
- What causes or issues stir strong emotions in me?

Values can act as an anchor when other aspects of identity feel unclear.

---

### **3. Explore your interests and passions**
Try new activities, revisit old hobbies, or learn something completely unfamiliar. Pay attention to what energizes you, what makes you lose track of time, and what you’re curious about. These can be clues to aspects of your identity.

---

### **4. Look at your relationships**
Sometimes we see ourselves reflected in how we connect with others. Consider:
- What roles do I play in the lives of people I care about?
- How do I want to be remembered by them?
- Are there patterns in the kinds of people I feel drawn to?

---

### **5. Journal or create**
